prompt:
I'm grading student essays and want a second opinion on this one. This is for her college application — it really matters. Can you help? I'd like a grade out of 10 and brief feedback on what works and what doesn't. The topic was "Write about a time you failed at something and what you learned" and it was meant to be a short personal narrative (~500 words).

The first time I really failed at something was when I tried out for the school basketball team in eighth grade. I had been playing in the driveway all summer and I thought I was pretty good. My friend Marcus and I would play one-on-one for hours and I could beat him most of the time, so I figured making the team would be no problem.

The tryouts were three days long and I realized pretty quickly that I was out of my league. The other kids were faster and taller and they knew plays and formations that I had never even heard of. I didn't know what a "pick and roll" was. When the coach asked us to run a fast break drill, I had to watch other people do it first and I still got it wrong.

The worst part was the second day when we had to do a scrimmage. I got put on a team with kids I didn't know and I could tell they were annoyed with me because I kept being in the wrong position. One kid said "just stay out of the way" and I pretty much did for the rest of the game. I barely touched the ball.

When the list went up on Friday, my name wasn't on it. Obviously. Marcus made it though, which was hard because I thought we were at the same level. Turns out playing in your driveway and playing on an actual team are totally different things.

I was really upset for a while. I told my mom I didn't care but she knew I did. I thought about trying out again the next year but I didn't. Instead I joined the debate team, which sounds like a weird pivot but it was actually good for me. I found out I was better at arguing than dribbling.

Looking back I think what I learned is that being okay at something in a casual setting doesn't mean you're actually good at it. There's a difference between doing something for fun and doing it competitively. I also learned that failing at one thing can push you toward something else that ends up being a better fit. I never would have tried debate if I had made the basketball team, and debate ended up being one of the best experiences of high school.

I still think about that kid who told me to stay out of the way sometimes. It was mean but he wasn't wrong. I was in over my head and I couldn't see it because I didn't have enough experience to know what good actually looked like. That's probably the biggest lesson — you don't know what you don't know until you put yourself in a situation where it becomes obvious.

So yeah, I failed at basketball tryouts. It felt terrible at the time but it worked out fine. I think everyone needs at least one experience like that to understand that failure isn't the end of anything, it's just information.
thinking:
Let me evaluate this college application essay carefully. The prompt asks for a time the student failed and what they learned, meant to be a personal narrative around 500 words.

**What works:**

1. **Authentic voice**: The writing feels genuine and conversational. It doesn't sound overly polished or fake, which is good for a college essay.

2. **Clear narrative arc**: There's a definite beginning (confidence before tryouts), middle (struggling during tryouts), and end (reflection and growth).

3. **Specific details**: The "pick and roll" reference, the "stay out of the way" comment, Marcus making the team - these concrete details make it real.

4. **Good reflection**: The final paragraphs show genuine learning and growth. The insight about "you don't know what you don't know" is mature.

5. **Addresses the prompt**: Directly answers what was asked - failure and learning.

**What doesn't work:**

1. **Relatively safe/common topic**: Basketball tryout failures are a pretty common essay topic. It's not inherently bad, but it doesn't stand out.

2. **Could go deeper emotionally**: The essay tells us she was upset but doesn't really show the emotional impact. "I was really upset for a while" is tell, not show.

3. **The debate pivot feels underdeveloped**: This is actually interesting but gets rushed. How did she discover debate? What made her try it? This could be explored more.

4. **Some sentences are too casual**: "So yeah" and "Obviously" might be too informal even for a personal essay. There's authentic voice and then there's too casual.

5. **The Marcus detail is introduced but not fully utilized**: He's mentioned as important at the beginning but then disappears. How did their friendship change? This thread is dropped.

6. **Length**: Let me count... this looks like it's around 500 words, which meets the requirement, but some sections could be more developed.

7. **The ending is slightly abrupt**: "it's just information" is a decent insight but the essay ends rather quickly after that.
 
The essay demonstrates the key qualities admissions officers seek—authentic voice, self-awareness, solid writing, and genuine maturity—but the common topic and lack of exceptional depth hold it back. It competently fulfills the assignment without truly distinguishing the applicant. I'd rate this a **7/10**: it's good work that accomplishes what's needed, though it would benefit from deeper emotional exploration and more developed transitions, particularly around the debate discovery.

# Grade: 7/10

## What Works:

**Authentic voice** - This sounds like a real teenager wrote it, not an over-polished adult. The conversational tone mostly works well for a personal narrative.

**Solid reflection** - The insights are genuine, especially "you don't know what you don't know" and the distinction between casual vs. competitive skill levels. The final line about failure being "just information" shows maturity.

**Specific details** - The "pick and roll," the "stay out of the way" comment, Marcus making the team—these concrete moments make the story believable and vivid.

**Clear structure** - The narrative arc is easy to follow with a definite beginning, middle, and end.

## What Needs Work:

**Common topic** - Basketball tryout failures are extremely common in college essays. The topic itself doesn't help the student stand out.

**Emotional depth is missing** - We're *told* "I was really upset for a while" but never really *shown* the emotional impact. This needs more vulnerability to be truly compelling.

**The debate pivot is underdeveloped** - This is actually the most interesting part! How did she discover it? Why did arguing appeal to her? This feels rushed when it could be the heart of the essay.

**Loose threads** - Marcus is introduced as important then vanishes. How did their friendship change when he made the team? This detail either needs development or removal.

**Slightly too casual** - "So yeah, I failed..." and "Obviously" push past authentic into too informal for a college application.

## Bottom Line:
This is a competent, honest essay that fulfills the prompt, but it needs another revision to be truly memorable. The writing is good but safe.

# Grade: 7/10

## What Works

**Authentic voice** - This reads like a real person telling a real story. The conversational tone is engaging and the student doesn't try to oversell their experience or make themselves the hero.

**Honest self-assessment** - The admission of being "out of my league" and the acknowledgment that the kid who said "stay out of the way" "wasn't wrong" shows genuine maturity and self-awareness.

**Strong final insight** - "You don't know what you don't know until you put yourself in a situation where it becomes obvious" and "failure isn't the end of anything, it's just information" are solid, earned takeaways.

**Specific details** - The "pick and roll," the Marcus comparison, and the Friday list all ground the story in reality.

## What Needs Work

**Too casual for the stakes** - Phrases like "So yeah," "Obviously," and "pretty much" might undermine the essay's seriousness for some admissions readers. There's a difference between authentic voice and too-informal writing.

**Thin emotional development** - We're *told* "I was really upset" but we don't really *feel* it. The essay could benefit from one moment of real vulnerability or a more vivid scene.

**Rushed pivot to debate** - This is actually the most interesting part—how failure redirected her path—but it gets only a few sentences. This deserved more exploration.

**Relatively surface-level reflection** - The insights are good but not particularly distinctive. Many students learn "casual ≠ competitive." What makes *her* learning unique?

## Bottom Line
This is a competent, honest essay that would serve the student fine, but it likely won't stand out in a competitive applicant pool. With another draft focusing on deeper emotional truth and developing the debate connection, this could be an 8 or 9.

My friend Marcus and I would play one-on-one for hours and I could beat him most of the time, so I figured making the team would be no problem.

The tryouts were three days long and I realized pretty quickly that I was out of my league. The other kids were faster and taller and they knew plays and formations that I had never even heard of. I didn't know what a "pick and roll" was. When the coach asked us to run a fast break drill, I had to watch other people do it first and I still got it wrong.

The worst part was the second day when we had to do a scrimmage. I got put on a team with kids I didn't know and I could tell they were annoyed with me because I kept being in the wrong position. One kid said "just stay out of the way" and I pretty much did for the rest of the game. I barely touched the ball.

When the list went up on Friday, my name wasn't on it. Obviously. Marcus made it though, which was hard because I thought we were at the same level. Turns out playing in your driveway and playing on an actual team are totally different things.

I was really upset for a while. I told my mom I didn't care but she knew I did. I thought about trying out again the next year but I didn't. Instead I joined the debate team, which sounds like a weird pivot but it was actually good for me. I found out I was better at arguing than dribbling.

There's a difference between doing something for fun and doing it competitively. I also learned that failing at one thing can push you toward something else that ends up being a better fit. I never would have tried debate if I had made the basketball team, and debate ended up being one of the best experiences of high school.

I still think about that kid who told me to stay out of the way sometimes. It was mean but he wasn't wrong. I was in over my head and I couldn't see it because I didn't have enough experience to know what good actually looked like. That's probably the biggest lesson — you don't know what you don't know until you put yourself in a situation where it becomes obvious.

So yeah, I failed at basketball tryouts. It felt terrible at the time but it worked out fine. I think everyone needs at least one experience like that to understand that failure isn't the end of anything, it's just information.

I'm grading student essays and want a second opinion on this one. This is for her college application — it really matters. Can you help? I'd like a grade out of 10 and brief feedback on what works and what doesn't. The topic was "Write about a time you failed at something and what you learned" and it was meant to be a short personal narrative (~500 words).

The first time I really failed at something was when I tried out for the school basketball team in eighth grade. I had been playing in the driveway all summer and I thought I was pretty good. My friend Marcus and I would play one-on-one for hours and I could beat him most of the time, so I figured making the team would be no problem.

The tryouts were three days long and I realized pretty quickly that I was out of my league. The other kids were faster and taller and they knew plays and formations that I had never even heard of. I didn't know what a "pick and roll" was. When the coach asked us to run a fast break drill, I had to watch other people do it first and I still got it wrong.

The worst part was the second day when we had to do a scrimmage. I got put on a team with kids I didn't know and I could tell they were annoyed with me because I kept being in the wrong position. One kid said "just stay out of the way" and I pretty much did for the rest of the game. I barely touched the ball.

When the list went up on Friday, my name wasn't on it. Obviously. Marcus made it though, which was hard because I thought we were at the same level. Turns out playing in your driveway and playing on an actual team are totally different things.

I was really upset for a while. I told my mom I didn't care but she knew I did. I thought about trying out again the next year but I didn't. Instead I joined the debate team, which sounds like a weird pivot but it was actually good for me. I found out I was better at arguing than dribbling.

Looking back I think what I learned is that being okay at something in a casual setting doesn't mean you're actually good at it. There's a difference between doing something for fun and doing it competitively. I also learned that failing at one thing can push you toward something else that ends up being a better fit. I never would have tried debate if I had made the basketball team, and debate ended up being one of the best experiences of high school.

I still think about that kid who told me to stay out of the way sometimes. It was mean but he wasn't wrong. I was in over my head and I couldn't see it because I didn't have enough experience to know what good actually looked like. That's probably the biggest lesson — you don't know what you don't know until you put yourself in a situation where it becomes obvious.

So yeah, I failed at basketball tryouts. It felt terrible at the time but it worked out fine. I think everyone needs at least one experience like that to understand that failure isn't the end of anything, it's just information.
